title: Top Medigap Plans: Unbiased Reviews & Best Medicare Supplement Companies for 2026
rawContent:
What Are Medicare Supplement Plans and Why Do They Matter?

Roughly 14 million Americans currently hold Medigap policies, yet millions more are enrolled in Original Medicare without realizing how much cost exposure they carry. The 2026 Medicare Part A hospital deductible stands at $1,736 per benefit period, and the Part B annual deductible is $283. A serious illness or unexpected hospitalization can trigger these costs multiple times in a single year. Medicare supplemental insurance exists specifically to close those gaps.

Medigap plans are federally standardized by letter (A, B, D, G, K, L, M, and N) under guidelines set by the National Association of Insurance Commissioners (NAIC). That standardization is significant: the benefits within Plan G are legally identical regardless of which company sells it. What differs between insurers is the monthly premium, rate increase history, pricing methodology, and quality of customer service.

With more than 67 million Americans enrolled in Medicare overall, choosing the right Medigap provider is a high-stakes decision that affects your finances for potentially decades. This article goes beyond a simple ranked list, it gives you the framework to evaluate financial strength, pricing structure, and rate stability so you can choose the best Medicare supplement company for your specific situation.

To understand the full range of Medicare benefits before selecting a supplement plan, it helps to start with a clear picture of what Original Medicare does and does not cover.

How to Compare Medicare Supplement Companies: What Really Matters

Not all Medigap providers are created equal, even when they sell the exact same plan letter. Before you settle on a company, evaluate these five criteria:

  • AM Best financial strength rating: Look for an A rating or better, which signals strong long-term claims-paying ability, critical for a policy you may hold for 20 or more years.

  • Pricing methodology: Community-rated, issue-age-rated, and attained-age-rated structures each have different long-term cost implications (more on this below).

  • Rate increase history: How much has this company raised premiums on this specific plan over the past three to five years? This single data point often predicts future costs better than today's premium.

  • Household and pay discounts: Many insurers offer 5–12% discounts for spouses or domestic partners on the same policy, or for paying annually.

  • Customer service reputation: NAIC complaint index scores and J.D. Power rankings provide a useful baseline for comparing service quality across carriers.

The most overlooked factor is pricing methodology. Attained-age-rated plans often start with lower premiums but can increase substantially as you age. Community-rated plans charge the same premium regardless of age, which can offer greater long-term stability even if the starting premium is slightly higher. As David Lipschutz of the Medicare Rights Center notes, "Medigap plans are standardized by federal law, meaning the benefits within each plan letter are identical regardless of which insurance company sells it, the key differentiators are premium, price stability history, and customer service."

Medicare.gov's Medigap Policy Search tool lets you compare all available plans and companies by ZIP code side by side, an unbiased starting point before you dig deeper.

Medicare Supplement Coverage Options: Understanding the Plan Letters

For those who became Medicare-eligible on or after January 1, 2020, standardized Medigap plan letters in 2026 include A, B, D, G, K, L, M, and N. Each covers a different combination of Medicare cost-sharing gaps. For most new enrollees, the decision comes down to three plans: Plan G, Plan N, and high-deductible Plan G.

Plan F once the gold standard for comprehensive coverage, is no longer available to beneficiaries who became Medicare-eligible after January 1, 2020. It remains available to those eligible before that date. Plan F covers the $283 Part B deductible that Plan G does not.

Plan N offers a lower monthly premium than Plan G while still covering the Part A deductible. The trade-off: copays of up to $20 for office visits and up to $50 for emergency room visits that do not result in inpatient admission. For healthier beneficiaries who rarely visit the doctor, this can be an attractive middle-ground option.

High-deductible Plan G has a 2026 deductible threshold of $2,950 before coverage kicks in. If your standard Plan G premium is $150/month and a high-deductible version costs $50/month, you save $1,200 annually in premiums, meaning you would need to reach the full $2,950 deductible before the high-deductible plan becomes costlier. For healthy beneficiaries, this plan offers meaningful catastrophic protection at the lowest monthly cost.

For a detailed side-by-side comparison, see Medicare Supplement Plan F vs. Plan G vs. Plan N.

Plan G Benefits: The Most Popular Choice for New Enrollees

Plan G has emerged as the benchmark for new Medicare enrollees. Here is what it covers in 2026:

  • Part A hospital deductible: $1,736 per benefit period

  • Part A coinsurance and hospital costs for up to 365 additional days after Medicare benefits are exhausted

  • Part B coinsurance or copayment (after you pay the $283 deductible)

  • Skilled nursing facility coinsurance: $217 per day for days 21–100

  • Part B excess charges (amounts above Medicare-approved costs)

  • Foreign travel emergency care (up to plan limits)

Your maximum out-of-pocket exposure under Plan G in 2026 is just $283, the Part B deductible it does not cover. Medicare expert Philip Moeller puts it simply: "Plan G has become the most popular comprehensive option for new enrollees since Plan F became unavailable to those newly eligible after 2020."

You can explore Plan G details including pricing methodology and enrollment timing to make the most informed decision.

Plan F vs. Plan G: What's the Difference in 2026?

The only coverage difference between Plan F and Plan G is the $283 Part B annual deductible. Plan F pays it; Plan G does not. If a Plan F premium in your area costs more than $283 per year above a comparable Plan G premium, Plan G delivers equal or better value. For most beneficiaries newly eligible for Medicare, Plan F is not even an option, making this comparison most relevant for those who became eligible before January 1, 2020 and are reconsidering their current plan.

Top Medicare Supplement Companies for 2026: Unbiased Reviews

Premiums for the same plan letter can vary by hundreds of dollars annually between companies in the same ZIP code. Brand recognition alone is not a sufficient reason to choose an insurer. As Mary Beth Donahue, President and CEO of AHIP, puts it: "Consumers shopping for Medicare coverage need to carefully evaluate both the breadth of coverage and the long-term financial stability of the insurer, not just the monthly premium."

Below is an overview of the most widely available and consistently reviewed top Medigap insurance providers for 2026, evaluated on financial strength, plan availability, pricing structure, and notable features.

One differentiating note most best-of lists skip: strong regional and lesser-known carriers sometimes offer lower premiums or more stable rate histories in specific states. A licensed independent broker can surface these options, which you may never find by Googling a national list.

Mutual of Omaha Medicare Supplement

Mutual of Omaha consistently earns an AM Best A+ (Superior) financial strength rating and offers broad plan availability across most states. Its Plan G and Plan N pricing are frequently competitive, and the company offers household discounts that can reduce premiums by up to 7–12% when two members of a household are both insured.

Mutual of Omaha's rate increase history is generally considered stable relative to industry peers, making it a frequently recommended option for beneficiaries who prioritize long-term premium predictability. Underwriting is typically straightforward, and customer service scores are consistently above average. Explore Mutual of Omaha Medigap plans to review current offerings by state.

AARP/UnitedHealthcare (AM Best A as of [Date of Retrieval]) Medicare Supplement

AARP-branded Medigap plans are underwritten by UnitedHealthcare, one of the largest health insurers in the country. A key structural advantage in many states: AARP/UnitedHealthcare uses a community-rated pricing model, meaning premiums do not increase solely because you age, a meaningful benefit for long-term cost stability.

The company carries a strong AM Best rating, a large national network of agents, and the added value of AARP membership resources. The trade-off is that starting premiums may be higher than some competitors in certain ZIP codes. Whether the community-rated structure offsets that difference over time depends on how long you hold the policy and where you live. You can review

com/medicare-supplements/medigap-by-carrier/united-healthcare-medigap-plans/">UnitedHealthcare Medigap plans for a deeper look.

Other Leading Providers: Cigna, Aetna, and Humana (AM Best A- as of [Date of Retrieval])

Cigna/Evernorth, Aetna, and Humana round out the national field of well-rated Medigap carriers. All three carry strong AM Best ratings and offer Plan G and Plan N in most states. Some of their supplement policies come with supplemental perks, gym memberships, telehealth access, or dental and vision add-ons, that may appeal to certain beneficiaries.

Keep in mind: those extras are supplemental perks, not standardized Medigap coverage. The federally standardized benefits within each plan letter are identical across all carriers. Evaluate Cigna, Aetna, and Humana on the same core criteria: premium, rate stability, AM Best rating, and customer service scores, then weigh any additional features as secondary considerations.

Medicare Supplement Plan Ratings: How to Evaluate Financial Strength

An AM Best financial strength rating reflects an insurer's long-term ability to pay claims. For a policy you may hold for 20 or 30 years, this matters. Look for a rating of A or better as a baseline filter when evaluating any Medigap company.

You can verify current AM Best ratings directly at ambest.com at no cost. Pair that with the NAIC Consumer Insurance Search tool, which provides state-level complaint ratios, a useful indicator of how well a company handles customer service issues relative to its market share.

J.D. Power's annual Medicare supplement satisfaction study adds a consumer experience dimension to the financial data. Used together, AM Best ratings and NAIC complaint indexes give you a two-dimensional view of insurer quality that no single source provides on its own.

A word of caution: aggregated "star ratings" on third-party review websites can be outdated, cherry-picked, or based on unverified submissions. Always verify medicare supplement plan ratings through primary, authoritative sources before making a decision.

Best Medicare Supplement Companies With No Network Restrictions

One of the most underappreciated advantages of any standardized Medigap plan is this: they work with any doctor or hospital in the United States that accepts Medicare. No networks. No referrals. No prior authorizations for covered services.

This stands in direct contrast to Medicare Advantage plans, which typically operate within HMO or PPO networks. In 2026, the maximum out-of-pocket limit for Medicare Advantage plans is $9,250 and that exposure only applies within network. Out-of-network costs can be higher or uncovered entirely depending on plan type.

Tricia Neuman of KFF frames the stakes clearly: "Medicare beneficiaries face meaningful differences in out-of-pocket costs depending on whether they choose original Medicare with a supplement or a Medicare Advantage plan, and those differences can add up to thousands of dollars annually."

The freedom from network restrictions carries particular value for beneficiaries who travel frequently, maintain long-standing relationships with specialist physicians outside a local area, or live in rural markets where Medicare Advantage plan options are limited. With Medigap, your coverage travels with you, anywhere Medicare is accepted nationwide.

How to Compare Medicare Supplement Plans and Rates in Your Area

Comparing Medigap options does not have to be complicated. Follow these steps:

  1. Identify the right plan letter. Match your health usage patterns and budget to the appropriate plan. Plan G for comprehensive coverage, Plan N for a lower premium with moderate cost-sharing, high-deductible Plan G for catastrophic-only protection at the lowest monthly cost.

  2. Use Medicare.gov's Medigap Plan Finder. Enter your ZIP code to see every company offering that plan in your area, with current premium estimates. This is the most unbiased starting point available.

  3. Request rate increase history. Ask each insurer, or your broker, how much premiums on this specific plan have increased over the past five years. A company with a lower premium today but a history of 8–10% annual increases may cost significantly more in five years than a competitor charging slightly more now.

  4. Work with a licensed independent broker. An independent broker can access multiple carriers simultaneously, provide rate history comparisons, and surface regional carriers that may offer more competitive pricing in your specific market.

Enrollment timing is critical. During your Medigap Open Enrollment Period the 6-month window starting when you enroll in Medicare Part B at age 65 or older, insurers cannot deny you coverage or charge higher premiums based on health conditions. This guaranteed issue protection is your strongest point. Outside of this window, most states require medical underwriting, and pre-existing conditions can result in higher premiums or outright denial.

For a deeper look at how to compare Medicare supplements in your area, including state-specific rules and tips for evaluating rate histories, additional resources are available.

Choosing the Best Medigap Provider for Your Needs: A Practical Summary

The best Medicare supplement company for you is not necessarily the one with the highest brand recognition or the lowest advertised premium. The right choice depends on your health, your location, your budget, and your timeline.

Start with the plan letter. Plan G covers the most ground for new enrollees, with a maximum out-of-pocket exposure of just $283 in 2026. Plan N suits beneficiaries who use healthcare services less frequently and can absorb modest copays. High-deductible Plan G works well for healthy beneficiaries who want the security of catastrophic protection without a high monthly premium.

Then evaluate companies on AM Best rating, pricing methodology, and rate stability. A plan that is the best value in one ZIP code may be significantly more expensive than a regional competitor across town. That is why personalized comparison, through Medicare.gov or a licensed independent broker, is not optional. It is the only way to know what you are actually paying for.

Getting a personalized comparison costs nothing and typically takes only a few minutes. Reviewing your options with a licensed agent ensures you have accurate, current 2026 pricing and rate history before you commit to a plan. To learn more about choosing a Medigap policy and what questions to ask during the process, consult available guidance before your Open Enrollment window closes.

Frequently Asked Questions About the Best Medicare Supplement Companies

What is the best Medicare supplement plan in 2026?

Plan G is widely considered the most comprehensive option available to new Medicare enrollees in 2026. It covers the $1,736 Part A deductible, Part B coinsurance, skilled nursing facility coinsurance, and Part B excess charges, leaving a maximum out-of-pocket exposure of only $283 (the Part B deductible). Philip Moeller, a recognized Medicare expert, identifies Plan G as the clear benchmark for new enrollees since Plan F's availability was restricted.

What is the difference between Medicare Supplement Plan F and Plan G?

The single difference is that Plan F covers the $283 Part B annual deductible in 2026; Plan G does not. Plan F is only available to beneficiaries who became Medicare-eligible before January 1, 2020. If a Plan F premium in your area exceeds the comparable Plan G premium by more than $283 per year, Plan G offers equal or better value.

Which Medicare supplement companies have the best ratings?

Mutual of Omaha (AM Best A+), AARP/UnitedHealthcare (AM Best A), Cigna/Evernorth, Aetna, and Humana are among the most consistently well-rated Medigap carriers nationally. Always verify current AM Best ratings at ambest.com and check NAIC complaint indexes for your specific state, since ratings and service quality can vary by region.

How much does Medicare Supplement insurance cost per month?

Plan G premiums for a 65-year-old can range from under $100 to over $200 per month depending on the state, insurer, pricing methodology, and personal factors like gender and tobacco use. National averages are less useful than a personalized quote, use Medicare.gov or a licensed broker to get accurate pricing for your ZIP code.

Is AARP/UnitedHealthcare Medicare Supplement worth it?

AARP/UnitedHealthcare is one of the largest Medigap providers nationally, with strong financial ratings and community-rated pricing in many states that supports long-term premium stability. That said, starting premiums can be higher than some competitors in certain ZIP codes, so comparing it against other available options before enrolling is always worthwhile.

Can I switch Medicare supplement plans at any time?

Outside of guaranteed issue periods, like the 6-month Open Enrollment window when you first enroll in Medicare Part B, switching Medigap plans in most states requires passing medical underwriting. Insurers can charge higher premiums or deny coverage based on health history. A handful of states (Connecticut, Massachusetts, and New York) offer additional consumer protections. Consult a licensed broker before attempting to switch outside a protected window.

Do Medicare supplement plans cover prescription drugs?

Medigap plans do not cover prescription drugs. For drug coverage, you must enroll separately in a Medicare Part D plan. In 2026, the average Part D premium is approximately $34.

50 per month with a maximum deductible of $615. Delaying enrollment triggers a permanent late penalty calculated on the $38. 99 base premium, so enrolling on time matters even if your current drug costs are low.

That is why personalized comparison, through Medicare.gov or a licensed independent broker, is not optional. It is the only way to know what you are actually paying for.

sections.calloutText: Getting a personalized comparison costs nothing and typically takes only a few minutes. Reviewing your options with a licensed agent ensures you have accurate, current 2026 pricing and rate history before you commit to a plan. To learn more about choosing a Medigap policy and what questions to ask during the process, consult available guidance before your Open Enrollment window closes.

sections.text: Frequently Asked Questions About the Best Medicare Supplement Companies

faqs.question: What is the best Medicare supplement plan in 2026?
faqs.answer: Plan G is widely considered the most comprehensive option available to new Medicare enrollees in 2026. It covers the $1,736 Part A deductible, Part B coinsurance, skilled nursing facility coinsurance, and Part B excess charges, leaving a maximum out-of-pocket exposure of only $283 (the Part B deductible). Philip Moeller, a recognized Medicare expert, identifies Plan G as the clear benchmark for new enrollees since Plan F's availability was restricted.

faqs.question: What is the difference between Medicare Supplement Plan F and Plan G?
faqs.answer: The single difference is that Plan F covers the $283 Part B annual deductible in 2026; Plan G does not. Plan F is only available to beneficiaries who became Medicare-eligible before January 1, 2020. If a Plan F premium in your area exceeds the comparable Plan G premium by more than $283 per year, Plan G offers equal or better value.

faqs.question: Which Medicare supplement companies have the best ratings?
faqs.answer: Mutual of Omaha (AM Best A+), AARP/UnitedHealthcare (AM Best A), Cigna/Evernorth, Aetna, and Humana are among the most consistently well-rated Medigap carriers nationally. Always verify current AM Best ratings at ambest.com and check NAIC complaint indexes for your specific state, since ratings and service quality can vary by region.

faqs.question: How much does Medicare Supplement insurance cost per month?
faqs.answer: Plan G premiums for a 65-year-old can range from under $100 to over $200 per month depending on the state, insurer, pricing methodology, and personal factors like gender and tobacco use. National averages are less useful than a personalized quote, use Medicare.gov or a licensed broker to get accurate pricing for your ZIP code.

faqs.question: Is AARP/UnitedHealthcare Medicare Supplement worth it?
faqs.answer: AARP/UnitedHealthcare is one of the largest Medigap providers nationally, with strong financial ratings and community-rated pricing in many states that supports long-term premium stability. That said, starting premiums can be higher than some competitors in certain ZIP codes, so comparing it against other available options before enrolling is always worthwhile.

faqs.question: Can I switch Medicare supplement plans at any time?
faqs.answer: Outside of guaranteed issue periods, like the 6-month Open Enrollment window when you first enroll in Medicare Part B, switching Medigap plans in most states requires passing medical underwriting. Insurers can charge higher premiums or deny coverage based on health history. A handful of states (Connecticut, Massachusetts, and New York) offer additional consumer protections. Consult a licensed broker before attempting to switch outside a protected window.

faqs.question: Do Medicare supplement plans cover prescription drugs?
faqs.answer: Medigap plans do not cover prescription drugs. For drug coverage, you must enroll separately in a Medicare Part D plan. In 2026, the average Part D premium is approximately $34.50 per month with a maximum deductible of $615. Delaying enrollment triggers a permanent late penalty calculated on the $38.99 base premium, so enrolling on time matters even if your current drug costs are low.
